name: New Zealand
summary: English-native with the best post-study runway of the set (a 3-year open work visa), a fast Green-List 'straight-to-residence' route for AI/ICT, world-class safety (GPI 3rd, WJP 5th) and a strong passport — but it carries a decisive blocker: New Zealand is NOT on Bangladesh's Dual Nationality Certificate list, so a naturalising Bangladeshi loses BD nationality (the explicit contrast to Australia). Compounded by a small, remote Auckland-centric market, high tuition and a tiny Bangladeshi community. Derived overall is above 60, but the dual-retention blocker governs.

links.title: Immigration New Zealand — official
New Zealand Government — citizenship requirements (official)

job-market.summary: ICT/AI roles are on the Green List with decent salaries, but the market is small, Auckland-centric and competes with Australia for talent.

pros.text: ICT/AI roles are on the Green List (straight-to-residence eligible); AI engineers earn ~NZ$100k–170k and senior software engineers ~NZ$130k–175k in Auckland, in an English-native market.

cons.text: The market is small and Auckland-centric, with ongoing brain-drain of tech talent to Australia.

links.title: Jobicy — AI Engineer salary, New Zealand (2025)
Levels.fyi — Software Engineer salary, Auckland

visa-access.summary: An online application process with no Bangladesh-specific ban, but New Zealand has no mission in Dhaka and funds requirements (NZ$20,000/yr) plus distance add friction.

pros.text: Student visas are filed online with no Bangladesh-specific ban; an English-taught degree needs no separate language friction.

cons.text: New Zealand has no mission in Bangladesh (processed via New Delhi/online), funds proof is NZ$20,000/yr, and the distance raises relocation cost.

links.title: Immigration New Zealand — student visa (official)

citizenship.summary: The decisive failure: although New Zealand allows dual citizenship, New Zealand is NOT on Bangladesh's DNC list, so a naturalising Bangladeshi loses BD nationality — the explicit contrast to Australia.

pros.text: Naturalisation needs only 5 years' residence (1,350 days) with basic English (native for this couple) and good character — low friction on New Zealand's side.
The New Zealand passport is strong (Henley 2026 ~183 visa-free destinations).

cons.text: New Zealand is NOT on Bangladesh's Dual Nationality Certificate list (Australia is; NZ is the explicit contrast), so under Bangladeshi law acquiring NZ citizenship causes loss of BD nationality — failing the retain-BD goal. (Recommend confirming against the current Bangladesh MOHA DNC list before relying on this.)

links.title: New Zealand Government — NZ citizenship requirements (official)
Bangladesh MOFA — Dual Nationality Certificate (eligible-country basis)

post-study-work.summary: A standout: a master's (Level 9) graduate gets a 3-year open Post-Study Work Visa, feeding the Green-List straight-to-residence route, with each spouse eligible.

pros.text: A Level-9 master's earns a 3-year open Post-Study Work Visa (any employer), and ICT/AI roles on the Green List offer a fast straight-to-residence path; each graduating spouse qualifies.

cons.text: Graduate-visa settings are being adjusted from November 2026 (a 6-month short-term variant added alongside the degree route).

links.title: Immigration New Zealand — Post Study Work Visa (official)

spouse-family.summary: Partners of skilled workers/students get work rights and children attend school free; if both spouses study, each holds an independent track.

pros.text: Partners of skilled-work and accredited-employer visa holders can obtain open work rights, children attend school free for residents, and if both spouses study each holds their own visa and PSW.

cons.text: Some partner-of-student work rights are conditional on the principal's study level.

links.title: Immigration New Zealand — partner & family visas (official)

msc-study.summary: English-native CS/AI master's (Auckland, Waikato) with a 3-year PSW payoff, but international tuition is high (NZ$32k–64k).

pros.text: Auckland and Waikato offer English-native Master of AI/CS programmes; a Level-9 master qualifies for the 3-year PSW and Green-List residence, and students may work 20 hrs/week.

cons.text: International master's tuition is high (NZ$32,000–64,000/yr) and the CS/AI catalogue is comparatively small.

links.title: University of Waikato — Master of Artificial Intelligence (official)
University of Auckland — international postgraduate fees (official)

pr-pathway.summary: A fast, clear route: ICT/AI roles on the Green List offer straight-to-residence, with English-only requirements.

pros.text: ICT/AI occupations on the Green List provide a fast 'straight-to-residence' or work-to-residence path, with only English required and clear points criteria.

cons.text: Immigration settings have been reset several times, adding some unpredictability, and continuity depends on Green-List stability.

links.title: Immigration New Zealand — Green List & residence (official)

income-cost.summary: Decent salaries with no social-security tax, but Auckland housing is among the least affordable globally and relocation distance is costly.

pros.text: No employee social-security tax and no capital-gains tax leave a reasonable net surplus on a dual tech income.

cons.text: Auckland housing is among the least affordable in the world, and high tuition plus distance make the study and relocation phases expensive.

links.title: Mastersportal — tuition & living costs in New Zealand (2026)

healthcare.summary: Publicly funded care for residents and 2-year+ work-visa holders; students need insurance and there are public waiting lists.

pros.text: Publicly funded healthcare covers residents and holders of 2-year+ work visas at low cost, plus ACC accident cover for everyone.

cons.text: International students must hold insurance, and the public system has notable waiting lists.

links.title: Immigration New Zealand — health & insurance (official)

culture-language.summary: English-native with no language barrier for status, a multicultural and (post-Christchurch) notably inclusive society — though remote.

pros.text: English is the native language (no language friction for work, PR or citizenship), and the society is multicultural with strong, demonstrated inclusion of the Muslim community since 2019.

cons.text: Geographic isolation makes travel to Bangladesh long and costly, and integration support is modest.

links.title: New Zealand Government — living in NZ (official)

safety-law.summary: Among the world's safest (GPI 3rd) with top-tier rule of law (WJP 5th) and strong, post-Christchurch anti-discrimination reforms.

pros.text: Global Peace Index 2025: 3rd; WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 5th — extremely safe, low corruption, with strengthened hate-speech/firearms and community-protection measures after the 2019 Christchurch attack.

cons.text: The 15 March 2019 Christchurch mosque attack remains the reference point, though the state and society responded with strong solidarity and reform.

links.title: Vision of Humanity — Global Peace Index 2025
World Justice Project — Rule of Law Index 2025

politics.summary: Stable democracy with a moderate populist (NZ First) coalition partner; immigration settings shift but the climate toward Muslims is notably inclusive post-2019.

pros.text: Very stable institutions, a Green-List policy that actively courts skilled ICT/AI migrants, and a demonstrably inclusive posture toward Muslims since 2019.

cons.text: Immigration settings are reset relatively often, and NZ First adds a populist edge to the governing coalition.

links.title: Immigration New Zealand — official

tax.summary: A relatively light burden — income tax to 39% with no employee social-security tax and no capital-gains tax — but no expat regime; an NZ–Bangladesh treaty exists.

pros.text: No employee social-security tax and no capital-gains tax keep the effective burden moderate, and an NZ–Bangladesh double-taxation treaty supports repatriation.

cons.text: There is no special expat/inbound-worker tax relief regime.

links.title: Inland Revenue (IRD) — income tax for individuals (official)

muslim-diaspora.summary: A ~1.5% Muslim population with abundant Auckland mosques and halal (NZ is a major halal-meat exporter), and a strongly accommodating legal posture since 2019 — but a tiny Bangladeshi community and geographic isolation.

pros.text: Auckland has 30+ mosques and halal is abundant (New Zealand is a major halal-meat exporter); the legal/social posture toward Muslims is strongly accommodating after 2019.

cons.text: The Bangladeshi community is tiny (~3,550) and the country is geographically isolated from Bangladesh.

links.title: Bangladeshi New Zealanders (overview)
